title: Mount Meru | Himalayan peak, Hinduism, Buddhism | Britannica
highlights: Meru, in Hindu mythology, a golden mountain that stands in the centre of the universe and is the axis of the world. It is the abode of gods, and its foothills are the Himalayas, to the south of which extends Bhāratavarṣa (“Land of the Sons of Bharata”), the ancient name for India. The roof tower crowning the shrine in a Hindu temple represents Meru. As the world axis, Mount Meru reaches down below the ground, into the nether regions, as far as it extends into the heavens. Also known as Sumeru, this mythical mountain is not just a geographical curiosity or a poetic metaphor. It is the cosmic axis, the spiritual  ...    For Buddhists across Asia, especially in the Khmer, Thai, Burmese, and Tibetan traditions, Mount Meru embodies the very  ...    In Buddhist cosmology, Mount Meru is a sacred mountain that stands at the center of the universe. It is not a physical place on Earth, but a mythical axis mundi—a central pillar connecting the heavens, the human realm, and the underworld. According to traditional Buddhist texts: Mount Meru rises 84,000 yojanas (a vast, symbolic measurement) into the sky and extends an equal distance below the  ... title: Mount Meru: The Celestial Axis in Hindu and Buddhist Cosmology
highlights:
Meru: The Celestial Axis in Hindu and Buddhist Cosmology
**Articles:**  ...    Mount Meru is often described as the cosmic axis of Hindu and Buddhist beliefs, a sacred mountain that holds a unique position in the cosmological frameworks of these religions. It is considered the dwelling place of the god Indra, the king of gods, and serves as the center of the universe, connecting the heavens, earth, and nether worlds. In Hindu cosmology, Mount Meru is depicted as a towering mountain surrounded by a vast ocean of milk. It is said to be the pivot point around which the heavens rotate, embodying the unification of the divine and the earthly realms. This  ...    The significance of Mount Meru extends beyond mere symbolism; it is also used as a metaphor for the spiritual journey  ... title: Mount Meru - Encyclopedia of Buddhism
highlights: Meru (Skt.; T. ri rab; C. Xumishan/Miaogaoshan 須彌山/妙高山), a.k.a. Sumeru, is a great mountain that forms the central axis of our world-system (lokadhātu), according to Buddhist cosmology.[1] On the flat summit of the mountain lies the "Heaven of the Thirty-Three" (Trāyastriṃśa), the home of  ...    Meru has four sides facing the cardinal directions, each of which is made of a different precious stone. Surrounding it are seven mountain ranges and the great ocean where the four great island continents lie: in the south, Jambudvīpa(our world); in the west, Godānīya; in the north, Uttarakuru; in the east, Pūrvavideha. Above it are the abodes of the devas of the kāmadhātu.[3]  ...    Mount Sumeru stands in the middle of the world as its axis and is eight leagues (yojana) high.
